MOTION FOR ATTORNEY FEES AND EXPENSES
Off Calendar Jul-09-2021 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES: APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Scott Kaufman, Esq. (408-727-8882; lemonatty@gmail.com) of Kaufman & Kavicky for Plaintiff.
Shelley Buchanan, Esq. (415-566-3548; ssbuchananlaw@gmail.com) for Defendant, Mr. Scooter, Inc.

Plaintiff SASHA SHERMAN'S motion for attorney's fees is granted.

The court rejects defendant's argument that the motion should be denied because it was filed in an untimely manner. The motion was timely served and there is no prejudice to defendant, who responded on the merits. (See Carlton v. Quint (2000) 77 Cal.App.4th 690, 697 ["It is well settled that the appearance of a party at the hearing of a motion and his or her opposition to the motion on its merits is a waiver of any defects or irregularities in the notice of motion. This rule applies even when no notice was given at all."].)

Plaintiff may seek fees because the CCP 998 offer that defendant accepted did not include a waiver of attorney's fees. (See Ritzenthaler v. Fireside Thrift Co. (2001) 93 Cal.App.4th 986, 991 ["A compromise agreement that expressly settles 'all damages and injunctive claims,' but nothing more, cannot reasonably be interpreted as including a settlement of the plaintiffs' right to recover attorney fees."]; On-Line Power, Inc. v. Mazur (2007) 149 Cal.App.4th 1079 [employee entitled to seek fees after CCP 998 offer].)

The Court awards $15,413.50 in fees and a 1.5 multiplier ($23,120.25.) Plaintiff is also entitled to $1,006.40 in costs (Total = $24,126.65.) Plaintiff is entitled to her reasonable fees based on Civil Code secs. 1794(d) [fees under Song-Beverly Act] and 1795.5 [obligation of distributor/seller of used goods]. (See also Gavaldon v. DaimlerChrysler Corp. (2004) 32 Cal.4th 1246, 1257 [Song-Beverly Act extends to used goods with express warranties]; Kaufman Decl., Ex. 1 [plaintiff's invoice referencing express warranty].)

Plaintiff's billing rates, hours, and claimed lodestar are reasonable. (See Ketchum v. Moses (2001) 24 Cal.4th 1122, 1132-1133.) The 1.5 multiplier is also reasonable given the contingent nature of this case. (See Glaviano v. Sacramento City Unified School District (2018) 22 Cal.App.5th 744, 751 [multiplier factors include "(1) the novelty and the difficulty of the questions involved, (2) the skill displayed in presenting them, (3) success or failure, (4) the extent to which the nature of the litigation precluded other employment by the attorneys, (5) the contingent nature of the fee award"].)

Order is signed.

Judge: Richard Ulmer, Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Eric Throne, CSR No. 7855, Email: eric.throne.csr@gmail.com, Tel: 707-305-8964. =(302/RBU)
